title: French silk pie
subtitle: Rich chocolate mousse filling in a biscuit crust
excerpt: A no-bake filling of whipped butter, chocolate, eggs, and cream piled into a biscuit crust and chilled until firm. Denser than a mousse, smoother than a ganache, the American dinner party chocolate pie.
sourceNotes: French silk pie is an American dessert credited to a 1951 Pillsbury Bake-Off prize-winning recipe. Despite the name, it has no French origin: the title refers to the silky texture of the filling rather than any national provenance. The recipe is consistent across American domestic cookery sources from the 1950s onward: beaten butter, sugar, chocolate, and whole eggs form the base, with whipped cream folded in or used as a topping.

content.text: The filling for French silk pie starts by beating softened butter with sugar until pale. Then you add melted chocolate, followed by whole eggs one at a time. Beat for several minutes after each egg. That long beating brings air into the mixture and creates the silky texture the pie is named for. The eggs are added raw and stay raw; the smooth texture comes from air, not heat. Use pasteurised eggs if you are serving anyone who shouldn't eat raw egg.

Each egg must be beaten for at least 5 minutes before the next is added. This is not optional: the beating is what emulsifies the fat with the egg proteins and creates the smooth, mousse-like texture. A recipe that adds all the eggs at once and beats briefly once will produce a grainy, broken filling.

attrs.prepNote: whipped to soft peaks, for topping

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Mix the biscuit crumbs from 200 g {{digestive-biscuits}}, 75 g melted {{unsalted-butter}}, and 2 tbsp {{caster-sugar}} until the crumbs are evenly coated. Press firmly into the base and up the sides of a 23 cm pie dish. Bake for 10-12 minutes until just set. Leave to cool completely.

Beat 175 g room-temperature {{unsalted-butter}} and 200 g {{caster-sugar}} together on high speed for 4-5 minutes until very pale and fluffy. Beat in the cooled melted {{dark-chocolate}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Add the {{whole-eggs}} one at a time, beating on high for exactly 5 minutes after each addition. The filling will become progressively silkier and more voluminous. The finished mixture should be smooth, glossy, and very aerated.
Pour the filling into the cooled crust and smooth the top. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight, until completely firm. Spoon or pipe whipped {{double-cream}} over the top before serving.
